prompt: Form the past participle: parlare → ____
explanation: Italian's first-conjugation verbs (the ones whose infinitive ends in -are) build their past participle by swapping the -are ending for -ato. Parlare gives parlato.

prompt: Complete the sentence: 'Ieri Marco ____ con il direttore.' (parlare)
explanation: When you talk about something that happened in the past, Italian usually uses the passato prossimo: an auxiliary plus a past participle. Parlare's default auxiliary is avere; the 3sg form is ha. Past participle parlato. Working: Marco ha parlato.
examiner_note: If the learner writes 'è parlato' they hit the participle markpoint but fail the auxiliary one, exposing an essere/avere choice error.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri sera noi ____ la pasta al ragù.' (mangiare)
explanation: Mangiare acts on something (here, la pasta), so it's a transitive verb. In the passato prossimo, transitives take avere as their auxiliary; the 1pl form is abbiamo. Past participle mangiato. Working: abbiamo mangiato.

prompt: Complete: 'Maria ____ in centro stamattina.' (lavorare)
explanation: Lavorare doesn't take a direct object, so it's an intransitive verb. But it's not a verb of motion or change of state, so it falls into the default avere group rather than the essere group. 3sg ha. Past participle lavorato. Working: Maria ha lavorato.
examiner_note: Lavorare is a common 'gotcha': learners over-extend essere to all intransitives.

prompt: Form the past participle: comprare → ____
explanation: Comprare is a first-conjugation verb, so its past participle follows the regular pattern: swap -are for -ato. Comprare gives comprato.

prompt: Form the past participle: vendere → ____
explanation: Italian's second-conjugation verbs (the ones whose infinitive ends in -ere) build their regular past participle by swapping -ere for -uto. Vendere gives venduto. Many second-conjugation verbs are irregular here, but vendere is one of the well-behaved ones.

prompt: Complete: 'Il mese scorso loro ____ la macchina vecchia.' (vendere)
explanation: Vendere acts on a direct object (la macchina), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 3pl hanno. Regular second-conjugation past participle venduto. Working: hanno venduto.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri tu ____ il regalo?' (ricevere)
explanation: Ricevere acts on something (here, il regalo), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 2sg hai. Regular second-conjugation past participle ricevuto. Working: hai ricevuto.

prompt: Complete: 'All'inizio io ____ alla tua storia, ma poi ho capito.' (credere)
explanation: Credere here takes a prepositional complement (alla tua storia), not a direct object — so it's intransitive. But it isn't a verb of motion or change of state, so it stays in the default avere group. 1sg ho. Regular past participle creduto. Working: ho creduto.

prompt: Form the past participle: dovere → ____
explanation: Dovere is a regular second-conjugation verb in its participle: -ere becomes -uto, so dovuto. It's also a modal verb, which means in compound tenses it may show modal auxiliary inheritance — taking the auxiliary of the infinitive it governs rather than its own default avere.

prompt: Form the past participle: dormire → ____
explanation: Italian's third-conjugation verbs (the ones whose infinitive ends in -ire) build their regular past participle by swapping -ire for -ito. Dormire gives dormito.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri sera Luca ____ otto ore.' (dormire)
explanation: Sleeping isn't going anywhere or changing into something, so dormire — though intransitive — falls into the default avere group rather than the essere one. 3sg ha. Regular third-conjugation past participle dormito. Working: Luca ha dormito.
examiner_note: Common over-application: 'è dormito' (treating any intransitive as essere).

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri sera io ____ il romanzo.' (finire)
explanation: Finire here acts on a direct object (il romanzo), so it's transitive and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1sg ho. Regular third-conjugation past participle finito. Working: ho finito.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri lei ____ tutto subito.' (capire)
explanation: Capire acts on a direct object (tutto), so it's transitive and takes avere. 3sg ha. The past participle is regular: capito. Note that capire follows the -isc- pattern in the present tense (capisco/capisci/capisce), but that doesn't show up in the past participle. Working: ha capito.

prompt: Form the past participle: sentire → ____
explanation: Sentire is a regular third-conjugation verb, so its past participle just swaps -ire for -ito. Sentire gives sentito.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Io ____ mangiato la pasta.'
explanation: Mangiare is acting on something (la pasta), so it's a transitive verb. Transitives take avere as their auxiliary in the passato prossimo. 1sg ho. Working: ho mangiato.
examiner_note: If the learner writes 'sono mangiato' (which would be passive 'I am eaten'), it's a transparent auxiliary-choice miss.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Loro ____ lavorato tutto il giorno.'
explanation: Lavorare doesn't take a direct object, so it's intransitive. But it isn't a verb of motion or change of state, so it falls into the default avere group. 3pl hanno. Working: hanno lavorato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Tu ____ comprato il pane?'
explanation: Comprare acts on a direct object (il pane), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 2sg hai. Working: hai comprato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Lei ____ studiato medicina.'
explanation: Studiare acts on something (medicina), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 3sg ha. Working: ha studiato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Noi ____ visto un bel film.'
explanation: Vedere acts on a direct object (un bel film), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1pl abbiamo. Working: abbiamo visto.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Voi ____ parlato con il professore?'
explanation: Parlare doesn't take a true direct object (you talk 'with' or 'to' someone, not 'someone'), so it's intransitive. But like most non-motion intransitives it falls into the default avere group. 2pl avete. Working: avete parlato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Marco ____ andato a Roma.'
explanation: Going somewhere is directed movement, so andare is a verb of motion. Verbs of motion take essere as their auxiliary in the passato prossimo. 3sg è. Working: è andato.
examiner_note: The classic over-application error is 'ha andato'; this catches it via must_not_include.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Loro ____ venuti ieri sera.'
explanation: Coming somewhere is directed movement, so venire is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3pl sono. Working: sono venuti.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Io ____ partito alle sette.'
explanation: Leaving is directed movement, so partire is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 1sg sono. Working: sono partito.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Maria ____ arrivata stanca.'
explanation: Arriving is directed movement, so arrivare is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3sg è. With essere, the past participle agrees with the subject; Maria is f.sg, so arrivata. Working: è arrivata.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Tu ____ uscito presto stamattina.'
explanation: Going out is directed movement, so uscire is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 2sg sei. Working: sei uscito.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Noi ____ entrati al cinema alle otto.'
explanation: Entering is directed movement, so entrare is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 1pl siamo. Working: siamo entrati.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Lui ____ sceso al piano terra.'
explanation: Going down is directed movement, so scendere — when used intransitively as here — is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3sg è. Working: è sceso. (Used transitively, 'ho sceso le scale' = I went down the stairs, scendere becomes an ambiguous auxiliary case.)
examiner_note: Scendere is one of the verbs that can take avere when used transitively ('ho sceso le scale'). Here it is intransitive with directional complement.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Voi ____ tornati a casa?'
explanation: Returning is directed movement, so tornare is a verb of motion and takes essere as its auxiliary. 2pl siete. Working: siete tornati.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Io mi ____ alzato presto.'
explanation: Alzarsi describes the subject doing something to itself (getting oneself up), so it's a reflexive verb. All reflexive verbs take essere as their auxiliary in the passato prossimo. 1sg sono. Working: mi sono alzato.
examiner_note: Common over-application of avere ('mi ho alzato') is caught by must_not_include.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Tu ti ____ lavato bene?'
explanation: Lavarsi means washing oneself, so it's a reflexive verb. All reflexive verbs take essere as their auxiliary. 2sg sei. Working: ti sei lavato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Maria si ____ vestita in fretta.'
explanation: Vestirsi means dressing oneself, so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3sg è. With essere, the past participle agrees with the subject; Maria is f.sg, so vestita. Working: si è vestita.

prompt: Complete: 'Noi ci ____ svegliati tardi stamattina.' (svegliarsi)
explanation: Svegliarsi means waking oneself up, so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere as its auxiliary. 1pl siamo. With essere, the past participle agrees with the subject; an m.pl 'noi' takes svegliati. Working: ci siamo svegliati.

prompt: Complete: 'Loro si ____ molto alla festa di sabato scorso.' (divertirsi, masc pl)
explanation: Divertirsi means enjoying oneself, so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3pl sono. With essere, the past participle agrees with the subject; an m.pl 'loro' takes divertiti. Working: si sono divertiti.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Lei si ____ addormentata sul divano.'
explanation: Addormentarsi means falling asleep (literally, putting oneself to sleep), so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3sg è. The past participle agrees with the subject; Lei is f.sg, so addormentata. Working: si è addormentata.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Il film mi ____ piaciuto molto.'
explanation: Piacere isn't a verb of motion, change of state, or a reflexive — but it still takes essere. (It's part of a small residual group: piacere, bastare, mancare, sembrare.) The grammatical subject is whatever is liked, here 'il film' (3sg), so è. Working: mi è piaciuto.
examiner_note: Anglophone learners often try 'ha piaciuto' on the model of 'I have liked' — must_not_include catches it.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'I tuoi consigli mi ____ bastati.'
explanation: Bastare belongs to the same residual group as piacere: it takes essere despite not being motion, change, or reflexive. The grammatical subject is 'i tuoi consigli' (m.pl), so sono. Working: mi sono bastati.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Ti ____ mancata Roma in questi mesi?'
explanation: Mancare (in the sense 'to be missed') patterns like piacere: it takes essere, and the grammatical subject is what is being missed. Here that's Roma (f.sg), so è. Working: ti è mancata.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'La situazione mi ____ sembrata strana.'
explanation: Sembrare belongs to the piacere residual group: it takes essere. The grammatical subject is whatever is seeming a certain way — here 'la situazione' (f.sg), so è. Working: mi è sembrata.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Marco ____ corso la maratona di Roma.' (correre)
explanation: Correre is one of Italian's ambiguous auxiliary verbs: it switches families depending on use. Here it acts on a direct object (la maratona — running an event), so it's transitive and takes avere. 3sg ha. Working: ha corso.
examiner_note: This pair (transitive avere vs intransitive essere) is the test of the ambiguous bucket.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Marco ____ corso a casa appena ha sentito la notizia.' (correre)
explanation: Correre is an ambiguous auxiliary verb. Here it describes directional motion (running to a place, not running an event), so it acts as a verb of motion and takes essere. 3sg è. Working: è corso a casa.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Lei ____ passata di qui ieri sera.' (passare)
explanation: Passare is an ambiguous auxiliary verb. When it describes motion through a place (passing by), it acts as a verb of motion and takes essere; the past participle agrees with the f.sg subject, so passata. Working: è passata. (Contrast 'ho passato l'esame' where passare is transitive.)

prompt: Form the past participle: fare → ____
explanation: Fare doesn't follow the regular -ere → -uto pattern; instead it's an example of stem expansion (fare comes from older facere). The past participle is fatto.

prompt: Complete: 'Nell'esame di ieri io ____ molti errori.' (fare)
explanation: Fare acts on a direct object (molti errori), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1sg ho. The past participle is the irregular fatto. Working: ho fatto.

prompt: Form the past participle: dire → ____
explanation: Dire is another stem expansion verb (from older dicere). The past participle is detto, not the regular *dito.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri tu ____ la verità al professore?' (dire)
explanation: Dire acts on a direct object (la verità), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 2sg hai. The past participle is the irregular detto. Working: hai detto.

prompt: Complete: 'Stamattina noi ____ il treno delle otto.' (prendere)
explanation: Prendere acts on a direct object (il treno), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1pl abbiamo. The past participle is irregular: preso, not the over-regularised *prenduto. Working: abbiamo preso.
examiner_note: Common over-regularisation: 'prenduto'.

prompt: Form the most common past participle of vedere: vedere → ____
explanation: Vedere has an irregular past participle: visto. The regular form veduto exists but feels archaic in most modern contexts, so it earns only partial credit.
examiner_note: Accept 'visto' as the target. Treat 'veduto' as unusual/dated; for a learner output, mark not as wrong but flag for review.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri lui ____ un bel film al cinema.' (vedere)
explanation: Vedere acts on a direct object (un bel film), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 3sg ha. The past participle is the irregular visto. Working: ha visto.

prompt: Form the past participle: scrivere → ____
explanation: Scrivere has an irregular past participle: scritto, not the over-regularised *scrivuto.

prompt: Complete: 'La settimana scorsa io ____ una lettera a mia nonna.' (scrivere)
explanation: Scrivere acts on a direct object (una lettera), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1sg ho. The past participle is the irregular scritto. Working: ho scritto.

prompt: Complete: 'L'estate scorsa lei ____ il libro in due giorni.' (leggere)
explanation: Leggere acts on a direct object (il libro), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 3sg ha. The past participle is the irregular letto. Working: ha letto.

prompt: Complete: 'Quando sono entrato, io ____ il cappello sul tavolo.' (mettere)
explanation: Mettere acts on a direct object (il cappello), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 1sg ho. The past participle is the irregular messo. Working: ho messo.

prompt: Complete: 'Marco ____ la finestra perché faceva caldo.' (aprire)
explanation: Aprire is a third-conjugation verb but its past participle is irregular: aperto, not the over-regularised *aprito. The verb acts on a direct object (la finestra), so it's transitive and takes avere. 3sg ha. Working: ha aperto.
examiner_note: The common over-regularised form is 'aprito'.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri sera loro ____ il negozio alle otto.' (chiudere)
explanation: Chiudere acts on a direct object (il negozio), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 3pl hanno. The past participle is the irregular chiuso. Working: hanno chiuso.

prompt: Complete: 'Io ____ a Milano nel 1990.' (nascere, masculine speaker)
explanation: Being born is becoming-something rather than acting on something, so nascere is a verb of change of state and takes essere as its auxiliary. 1sg sono. The past participle is irregular: nato (m.sg agreement with the masculine speaker). Working: sono nato.

prompt: Complete: 'Il suo gatto ____ ieri.' (morire)
explanation: Dying is becoming-something (no longer alive), so morire is a verb of change of state and takes essere as its auxiliary. 3sg è. The past participle is irregular: morto (m.sg agreement with il gatto). Working: è morto.

prompt: Complete: 'Sabato scorso loro ____ alla festa con i loro figli.' (venire, masc pl)
explanation: Coming somewhere is directed movement, so venire is a verb of motion and takes essere. 3pl sono. The past participle looks regular (venuto with a -uto ending) but the stem changes from veni- to venu-. With essere, the participle agrees with the m.pl subject: venuti. Working: sono venuti.
examiner_note: Common miss: 'venito' (over-regularising as -ire → -ito) or 'venuto' (correct stem but wrong agreement for plural subject).

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri io ____ a Roma per lavoro.' (essere, masc speaker)
explanation: Essere and stare share a single past participle: stato. Essere takes itself as its own auxiliary in compound tenses. 1sg sono. With essere, the participle agrees with the m.sg speaker: stato. Working: sono stato.
examiner_note: Anglophone learners may invent 'essuto' on analogy with -ere → -uto. Catch it.

prompt: Complete: 'Tu ____ troppo caffè stamattina.' (bere)
explanation: Bere acts on a direct object (troppo caffè), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere as its auxiliary. 2sg hai. The past participle shows stem expansion (bere comes from older bevere): bevuto. Working: hai bevuto.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of andare: 'Marco è ____ a Roma.'
explanation: When the auxiliary is essere, the past participle has to agree with the subject in gender and number. Marco is m.sg, so the participle takes the -o ending: andato.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of arrivare: 'Mio fratello è ____ tardi.'
explanation: With essere as the auxiliary, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Mio fratello' is m.sg, so the participle takes the -o ending: arrivato.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of andare: 'Maria è ____ a Roma.'
explanation: With essere as the auxiliary, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. Maria is f.sg, so the participle takes the -a ending: andata. The classic miss is leaving it in the default -o (andato).
examiner_note: The 'forgot to agree' miss is 'andato'.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of uscire: 'Mia sorella è ____ con gli amici.'
explanation: With essere as the auxiliary, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Mia sorella' is f.sg, so the participle takes the -a ending: uscita.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of arrivare: 'I ragazzi sono ____ insieme.'
explanation: With essere as the auxiliary, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'I ragazzi' is m.pl, so the participle takes the -i ending: arrivati.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of partire: 'Loro (i miei zii) sono ____ ieri.'
explanation: Partire is a verb of motion, so it takes essere as its auxiliary, and the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Loro (i miei zii)' is m.pl, so the participle takes the -i ending: partiti.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of tornare: 'Le ragazze sono ____ a casa tardi.'
explanation: With essere as the auxiliary, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Le ragazze' is f.pl, so the participle takes the -e ending: tornate. The f.pl ending is often the last agreement a learner gets right; the easy miss is the default-masculine -i (tornati).
examiner_note: The default-masculine miss here is 'tornati'.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of nascere: 'Le mie cugine sono ____ in Italia.'

markpoints.label: Agreement fem pl (-e), irregular participle stem

explanation: Nascere is a verb of change of state, so it takes essere as its auxiliary, and the past participle has to agree with the subject. The participle is irregular: stem nat-. 'Le mie cugine' is f.pl, so the ending is -e: nate.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of nascere: 'Maria e Giulia sono ____ nel 2000.'
explanation: Two female subjects make a feminine plural group, so the past participle has to take f.pl agreement with essere: nate. (If even one subject were masculine, the rule would switch to mixed-gender default and the ending would become -i.)

prompt: Complete with the correct form of andare: 'Marco e Anna sono ____ al mare.'
explanation: Marco (m) and Anna (f) form a mixed-gender plural subject. Italian's mixed-gender default is m.pl agreement on the past participle: -i. So andati, not andate, regardless of which subject comes first or how many of each.
examiner_note: A common modern-language-aware miss is to choose -e on equity grounds; the grammatical default in Italian is still -i.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of mangiare: 'Maria ha ____ la pasta al pomodoro.'
explanation: When the auxiliary is avere and the direct object follows the verb (la pasta), the past participle stays invariable in the default -o form: mangiato. The participle does not agree with the subject or with the following object. The miss is mistakenly agreeing with feminine Maria (mangiata).
examiner_note: Common miss: agreeing 'mangiata' with feminine subject Maria, or with feminine following object 'pasta'.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of comprare: 'Le ragazze hanno ____ i biglietti.'
explanation: When the auxiliary is avere and the direct object follows the verb (i biglietti), the past participle stays invariable in the default -o form: comprato. 'Le ragazze' is f.pl, but with avere the subject doesn't trigger participle agreement.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of vedere: 'I bambini hanno ____ un cartone animato.'

markpoints.label: Avere, no preceding DOP: invariable -o (irregular participle)

explanation: When the auxiliary is avere and the direct object follows the verb (un cartone animato), the past participle stays invariable in the default -o form: visto, even though it's an irregular participle. 'I bambini' is m.pl but with avere the subject doesn't trigger agreement.

prompt: Q: 'Hai visto Marco?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, l'ho ____.'
explanation: Here l' is the elided lo, the m.sg DOP referring to Marco. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: m.sg → -o, so visto. (The agreement is invisible here because the default ending is also -o, but the bucket still fires.)

prompt: Q: 'Hai visto Maria?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, l'ho ____.'
explanation: Here l' is the elided la, the f.sg DOP referring to Maria. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: f.sg → -a, so vista. The classic miss is leaving the participle in the default -o (visto).
examiner_note: Common miss: leaving 'visto' (failure to agree). Catches the core skill.

prompt: Q: 'Hai visto i ragazzi?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, li ho ____.'
explanation: Li is the m.pl DOP, referring to 'i ragazzi'. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: m.pl → -i, so visti.

prompt: Q: 'Hai visto le ragazze?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, le ho ____.'
explanation: Le is the f.pl DOP, referring to 'le ragazze'. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: f.pl → -e, so viste.

prompt: Q: 'Hai comprato le mele?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, le ho ____.' (comprare)
explanation: Le is the f.pl DOP, referring to 'le mele'. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: f.pl → -e, so comprate. (Note that comprare is a regular -are verb, but the agreement still applies.)

prompt: Q: 'Hai mangiato la pizza?' Complete the answer: 'Sì, l'ho ____.' (mangiare)
explanation: Here l' is the elided la, the f.sg DOP referring to 'la pizza'. When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: f.sg → -a, so mangiata.

prompt: Reorder these words to form a correct negative sentence: 'mangiato / ho / non / la pizza'
explanation: When you negate something in the passato prossimo, 'non' goes immediately before the auxiliary, not between the auxiliary and the past participle. Standard negation placement: non ho mangiato. Anglophones often produce 'ho non mangiato' on the model of 'have not eaten', which is wrong.
examiner_note: Anglophones often produce 'ho non mangiato' on the model of 'have not eaten'. Must_not_include catches it.

prompt: Complete with the negated auxiliary (two words): 'Ieri io ____ visto Marco.'
explanation: When you negate something in the passato prossimo, 'non' sits immediately before the auxiliary. So with 1sg ho, the negation placement gives 'non ho visto'. The two-word answer is non ho.

prompt: Reorder to form a correct negative sentence with 'ancora' (yet): 'Marco / mangiato / non / ha / ancora'

markpoints.label: Negation with adverb between auxiliary and participle

explanation: Two rules combine here. Negation placement puts 'non' immediately before the auxiliary. Adverb placement puts short adverbs like ancora between the auxiliary and the past participle. Working: Marco non ha ancora mangiato.
examiner_note: Position of 'ancora' is the secondary skill here; the primary skill is non-placement.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Ieri Marco ____ parlato con il direttore.' (parlare)
explanation: Italian compound tenses ask two things at once: pick the right auxiliary family (avere or essere), then conjugate it for the right person. Parlare takes avere; Marco is 3sg, so the form is ha. The full answer is ha. A learner who writes 'hanno' has the family right but missed the person agreement.
examiner_note: The family markpoint is the only place we use 6 any_phrases; justified because the bucket is intrinsically family-shaped.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Le ragazze ____ comprato i biglietti la settimana scorsa.' (comprare)
explanation: Comprare is a transitive verb, so it takes avere as its auxiliary. 'Le ragazze' is 3pl, so person agreement gives hanno. Working: hanno comprato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Stamattina io ____ mangiato un panino.' (mangiare)
explanation: Mangiare is a transitive verb, so it takes avere as its auxiliary. Io is 1sg, so person agreement gives ho. Working: ho mangiato.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Tu e Maria ieri sera ____ visto il film.' (vedere)

markpoints.label: Person/number agreement: 'tu e X' → voi (2nd pl)

explanation: When Italian combines tu with another person ('tu e X'), the resulting subject becomes voi (2pl) rather than third-plural — that's the tu-X collapse. Vedere takes avere, so person agreement gives avete. Working: avete visto.
examiner_note: The 'tu e X → voi' collapse trips up learners who instinctively pick a 3rd-person form because Maria is 3rd. The person-agreement bucket isolates this miss.

prompt: Fill in the auxiliary: 'Voi ieri ____ partiti alle sette.' (partire)
explanation: Partire is a verb of motion, so it takes essere. Voi is 2pl, so person agreement gives siete. The visible participle partiti is already in m.pl agreement, which signals essere territory and confirms the auxiliary choice. Working: siete partiti.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri Maria ____ voluta partire presto.' (volere + partire)
explanation: When a modal verb governs an infinitive that selects essere (partire is a verb of motion), prescriptive Italian shows modal auxiliary inheritance: the modal takes essere too. 3sg è. The participle voluta is already in f.sg agreement, confirming essere territory. Working: è voluta partire.
examiner_note: Colloquial Italian increasingly tolerates 'ha voluta partire' but written/formal Italian observes inheritance. Marker scores the prescriptive form only here.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri Marco ____ dovuto fare i compiti.' (dovere + fare)
explanation: Fare is a transitive verb that takes avere. With modal auxiliary inheritance, dovere inherits avere too: ha dovuto fare. The modal's participle stays in the default -o because avere doesn't trigger participle agreement with the subject. 3sg ha.

prompt: Complete: 'Loro ____ potuti partire in orario ieri.' (potere + partire)

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can

explanation: Partire is a verb of motion that takes essere. With modal auxiliary inheritance, potere inherits essere too: sono potuti partire. The m.pl participle potuti is already in agreement, confirming essere territory. 3pl sono.

prompt: Complete: 'Stamattina io ____ voluto mangiare la pasta.' (volere + mangiare)
explanation: Mangiare is a transitive verb that takes avere. With modal auxiliary inheritance, volere inherits avere too: ho voluto mangiare. 1sg ho.

prompt: Complete: 'Ieri sera lei ____ dovuta uscire prima.' (dovere + uscire)

translation.reveal: uscire - to go out / to leave

explanation: Uscire is a verb of motion that takes essere. With modal auxiliary inheritance, dovere inherits essere too: è dovuta uscire. The f.sg participle dovuta is already in agreement, confirming essere territory. 3sg è.

prompt: Place 'già' correctly: 'Marco ____ mangiato.' (mangiare)
explanation: Italian's adverb placement rule puts a small set of high-frequency adverbs (già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena) between the auxiliary and the past participle. So 'già' goes after ha and before mangiato: ha già mangiato. Anglophones often pre-pose ('già ha mangiato') or post-pose ('ha mangiato già') it; both are wrong.

prompt: Place 'ancora' correctly: 'Non ____ finito i compiti.' (finire, 1sg)
explanation: Even with negation in the mix, the adverb placement rule still applies: ancora goes between the auxiliary and the past participle. Negation placement puts 'non' before the auxiliary. Together: non ho ancora finito.

prompt: Place 'mai' correctly: 'Non sono ____ a Roma.' (andare or stare, masc sg)

markpoints.label: Adverb between auxiliary and participle (negative + essere)

explanation: Mai in the 'non...mai' construction (= never) follows the adverb placement rule: it sits between the auxiliary and the past participle. Either andato (from andare, motion) or stato (from stare or essere) works as the participle, depending on whether the speaker means going or being.
examiner_note: Two participles accepted; both fire the same bucket because the skill is adverb placement, not verb choice.

prompt: Place 'sempre' correctly: 'Lui ha ____ in Italia.' (vivere)
explanation: The adverb placement rule applies to sempre too: it sits between the auxiliary and the past participle. So ha sempre vissuto. Vivere has an irregular past participle: vissuto.

prompt: Place 'appena' correctly: 'Sono ____ a casa.' (tornare, masc sg)

translation.reveal: tornare - to return / to come back

explanation: Appena (= just) follows the adverb placement rule: it sits between the auxiliary and the past participle. So sono appena tornato.

prompt: Q: 'Hai mangiato delle mele?' Complete: 'Sì, ne ho ____ tre.' (mangiare)

markpoints.label: Agreement with implicit partitive referent (fem pl)

explanation: Here 'ne' is the partitive standing in for 'mele' (f.pl). When the preceding partitive ne sits before the verb, the past participle has to agree with the implicit referent: f.pl → -e, so mangiate. The miss is leaving the participle in the default -o (mangiato).
examiner_note: Common miss: 'ne ho mangiato tre' (no agreement). This is the diagnostic.

prompt: Q: 'Hai comprato dei libri?' Complete: 'Sì, ne ho ____ due.' (comprare)

markpoints.label: Agreement with implicit partitive referent (masc pl)

explanation: Here 'ne' is the partitive standing in for 'libri' (m.pl). When the preceding partitive ne sits before the verb, the past participle has to agree with the implicit referent: m.pl → -i, so comprati.

prompt: Q: 'Hai letto dei romanzi durante l'estate?' Complete: 'Sì, ne ho ____ molti.' (leggere)

markpoints.label: Agreement with implicit partitive referent (masc pl, irregular participle)

explanation: Here 'ne' is the partitive standing in for 'romanzi' (m.pl). When the preceding partitive ne sits before the verb, the past participle has to agree with the implicit referent: m.pl → -i. The participle is the irregular letto, which inflects regularly for agreement: letti.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of arrivare: 'Mio fratello e mia sorella sono ____ tardi ieri.'
explanation: Mio fratello (m) + mia sorella (f) makes a mixed-gender plural subject. Italian's mixed-gender default is m.pl agreement on the past participle: -i, so arrivati. The natural-feeling miss (arrivate) tries to track one of the two genders rather than defaulting to masculine.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of nascere: 'Mio padre e le mie sorelle sono ____ in Australia.'
explanation: Mio padre (m) + le mie sorelle (f.pl) is a mixed-gender plural subject. Italian doesn't count proportions: any masculine in the group triggers the mixed-gender default of m.pl agreement, so the participle takes -i. Nascere's irregular participle nato becomes nati. Working: sono nati.
examiner_note: Tempting miss: 'nate' on the grounds that two of the three subjects are female. Italian doesn't count; any masculine in the group triggers masc pl.

prompt: Complete with the correct form of partire: 'Anna e suo fratello sono ____ in vacanza la settimana scorsa.'
explanation: Anna (f) + suo fratello (m) is a mixed-gender plural subject. Italian's mixed-gender default gives m.pl agreement on the past participle: -i, so partiti.
